title: Exclusive-Nvidia’s AI chip sale to ByteDance hinges on conditions set by Trump administration
content:
By Karen Freifeld

Feb 4 (Reuters) - The Trump administration is willing to allow China’s ByteDance to buy Nvidia’s H200 chips, but the AI chipmaker has not agreed to proposed conditions for their use, according to a person familiar with the matter.

The U.S. said it would approve the license about two weeks ago, the person said, but Nvidia has not accepted the U.S. government’s Know-Your-Customer (KYC) requirement as now drafted - to ensure China’s military does not access the chips - among other conditions.

More broadly, Nvidia is negotiating with the U.S. over the terms of licenses to ship its H200 AI chips to companies in China, according to the person and two others familiar with the matter.

Nvidia said it was an intermediary between the U.S. government and potential customers that would have to comply with the U.S. restrictions.

"We aren’t able to accept or reject license conditions on our own," a company spokesperson said in a statement. "Although KYC is important, KYC is not the issue. For American industry to make any sales, the conditions need to be commercially practical, else the market will continue to move to foreign alternatives."

ByteDance, which owns TikTok and is one of the biggest AI companies in China, could not be immediately reached for comment. The Commerce Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Nvidia and its Chinese customers have once again found themselves caught in the crossfire of the U.S.-China tech war. China has already granted preliminary approval to three of its largest tech companies - ByteDance, Tencent, and Alibaba - along with AI startup Deepseek to import the chips, though the regulatory conditions for China’s approvals also are still being finalized.

This news from the U.S. side represents the latest twist on whether China will get chip shipments first promised by President Donald Trump in early December, with a 25 percent cut to the U.S. government. The same arrangement applies to similar chips from firms such as Advanced Micro Devices and Intel.

Trump’s decision was decried by China hawks, who viewed it as a risk to national security, and fear that China will use the chips to strengthen its military capabilities.

On January 15, the Commerce Department issued a regulation formally loosening the licensing policy for the chips, but stipulating conditions. For example, it said the applicant had to certify and provide data to support that its customer will use "rigorous" Know Your Customer procedures to screen and prevent unauthorized remote access. In addition, it is required to provide a list of remote users tied to companies in Iran, Cuba, Venezuela and other countries of concern.

Before the chips could go to China, a U.S. third-party lab also is required to test the chips to ensure they meet specifications, a requirement viewed by many as a way for the U.S. to collect its 25 percent fee.

The Commerce Department typically circulates licenses pending approval to other agencies with the proposed conditions, according to a former Commerce official. Once they agree, the conditions may be sent to the license applicant - in this case, Nvidia - essentially asking the company if they can live with them. The applicant may suggest changes, which would be sent back to the agencies involved for their agreement. The departments of State, Defense and Energy are included.

One source suggested at least some chips are likely to go to China by the time Trump plans to meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping in April.

title: Asia shares falter as global tech selloff spooks investors
content:
By Stella Qiu

SYDNEY, Feb 5 (Reuters) - Asian stocks faltered as concerns about the exploding costs of AI investment hounded the tech sector, though Wall Street futures were trying to make a comeback as chip providers gained.

Google parent Alphabet reported solid results after the bell, but it was targeting capital expenditure of $175 billion to $185 billion this year, sharply above analysts’ estimates. Shares swung wildly - down over 6% at one point -before settling just 0.4% lower after-hours.

Investors have been rotating out of technology giants and into cyclical stocks amid fears of AI disruptions to jobs. The recent selloff, triggered by a new legal tool from Anthropic’s Claude large language model, has wiped out about $830 billion in market value since January 28.

Disappointing earnings results from Advanced Micro Devices didn’t help either, with the chipmaker tumbling 17% overnight.

"That increase in (Alphabet) capex was absolutely enormous," said Tony Sycamore, analyst at IG.

"At a time when everyone is hyper-sensitive and hyper-nervous about what’s going on with the software companies, with what’s going on with CAPEX and AI valuations... I would have thought the reaction would be quite negative, but it’s not flowing through to the Nasdaq futures."

The demand for more equipment spending helped chip giant Nvidia rally almost 2% after the bell, partially reversing a 3.4% tumble overnight. That likely lifted Nasdaq futures 0.6% and S&P 500 0.4%.

Still, MSCI’s broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan skidded 1%. South Korea’s KOSPI tumbled 1.7% and Taiwanese shares lost 0.7%

Japan’s Nikkei was flat.

Chinese blue chips lost 0.7%, while Hong Kong’s Hang Seng index slid 0.8%.

Much attention is on Amazon’s results later in the day, as well as policy meetings from the Bank of England and the European Central Bank where rates are expected to be on hold.

YEN WEAK AHEAD OF JAPAN ELECTION

The Japanese yen nursed a fourth straight day of losses ahead of a general election on Sunday where polls are tipping a decisive victory for Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, endorsing her spending ambitions that have raised concerns about the nation’s strained finances.

The dollar was steady at 156.93 yen, having rallied 3% from its low of 152.1 yen when chatter that the New York Federal Reserve conducted rate checks sent the Japanese currency flying high.

In the Treasuries market, the benchmark 10-year yield was flat at 4.2715%. The U.S. nonfarm payrolls report for January has been pushed back from its scheduled release on Friday to February 11 due to a four-day partial government shutdown that has now ended.

Oil prices fell on Thursday after two straight days of gains as the U.S. and Iran agreed to hold talks in Oman on Friday, despite differences about the agenda. [O/R]

U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude fell 1.4% to $64.23 per barrel, while Brent crude futures also dropped 1.4% to $68.47 per barrel.

Gold and silver prices were up a little in early trade after an epic implosion last Friday that saw the two plunging from lofty record highs.

Gold rose 0.3% to $4,976 an ounce while silver inched up 0.2% to $88.20 an ounce.

title: Cathie Wood’s ARK sells Teradyne stock, buys AMD and Tesla
content:
Cathie Wood’s ARK ETF published their daily trades for Wednesday, February 4th, 2026, showcasing significant movements in their investment strategy. The largest transaction of the day was the sale of 258,001 shares of Teradyne Inc (NASDAQ:TER) across several ARK ETFs, totaling a substantial $73,009,122. This sale continues a recent trend as ARK has been offloading Teradyne shares throughout the week.

In a notable acquisition, ARK purchased 141,108 shares of Advanced Micro Devices Inc (NASDAQ:AMD) across multiple ETFs, with a total investment of $34,163,657. This buy signals ARK’s growing interest in the semiconductor sector, a move that investors will be keen to monitor.

Tesla Inc (NASDAQ:TSLA) also saw a significant purchase, with ARK acquiring 35,766 shares worth $15,091,821 through its ARKX ETF. This adds to ARK’s existing position in the electric vehicle giant, reflecting continued confidence in Tesla’s growth potential.

Other notable buys included 407,950 shares of Tempus AI Inc (TEM), valued at $22,832,961, and 36,065 shares of GeneDx Holdings Corp (WGS), totaling $3,434,469. Tempus AI’s purchase highlights ARK’s ongoing interest in artificial intelligence and biotechnology sectors.

On the selling side, ARK reduced its holdings in Global-E Online Ltd (NASDAQ:GLBE) by 190,830 shares, amounting to $6,469,137, and in Trade Desk Inc (NASDAQ:TTD) by 156,758 shares, totaling $4,260,682. These transactions suggest a strategic reallocation of resources within ARK’s diverse portfolio.

Additionally, ARK’s smaller trades included buying 37,379 shares of Kodiak AI Inc (KDK) and selling 107,092 shares of Pinterest Inc (NYSE:PINS), indicating a nuanced approach to tech investments.

Investors will be watching closely to see how these moves align with ARK’s broader investment strategy and market trends.

title: Wall Street ends down as AI worries slam tech stocks
content:
By Noel Randewich and Pranav Kashyap

Feb 4 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks ended lower on Wednesday, with losses in Advanced Micro Devices, Palantir and other technology companies, as investors worried about pricey valuations and whether Wall Street’s AI rally has reached its peak.

Alphabet fell almost 2% ahead of its quarterly results after the bell. After the close, it regained 2% as the company said it was aggressively ramping up spending as it deepens its investments in the AI race.

Advanced Micro Devices tumbled 17% after the chipmaker forecast quarterly revenue that disappointed investors and suggested it is having a tough time competing against AI heavyweight Nvidia.

Nvidia dropped 3.4% and the PHLX semiconductor index fell 4.4%.

Palantir slumped almost 12%, reversing sharp gains from the previous day that were driven by the AI data company’s strong quarterly sales.

"The size of the infrastructure buildout is unprecedented, and the pace of consumers and businesses adopting AI tools is also unprecedented. The stock market is having a really hard knowing where to price the stocks and what the future looks like. ... The market is suddenly skeptical and concerned about it," said Jed Ellerbroek, a portfolio manager at Argent Capital in St. Louis.

Some software companies added to recent losses amid worries that rapidly advancing AI could disrupt industry incumbents. Snowflake fell 4.6% and Datadog lost 3.3%.

"If you’ve got legacy software that’s old and clunky, you’re a ripe target for AI. We’re a bit bearish on software in general, with the whole impetus of AI," said Josh Chastant, portfolio manager, public investments at GuideStone Funds.

Investors selling AI-related stocks shifted into less pricey companies that sat out the tech rally in recent years. The S&P 500 value index gained for a fifth straight session, while the S&P 500 growth index dropped.

The S&P 500 declined 0.51% to end the session at 6,882.72 points.

The Nasdaq fell 1.51% to 22,904.58 points, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.53% to 49,501.30 points.

Even as the S&P 500 lost ground, seven of the 11 S&P 500 sector indexes rose, led by energy, up 2.25%, followed by a 1.8% gain in materials.

Volume on U.S. exchanges was heavy, with 24.6 billion shares traded, compared to an average of 19.9 billion shares over the previous 20 sessions.

Super Micro Computer’s shares jumped 13.8% after the company raised its annual revenue forecast on sustained demand for its AI-optimized servers as companies ramp up data center capacity.

Limiting losses in the S&P 500, shares of the drugmaker Eli Lilly rallied about 10% after the company forecast 2026 profit above Wall Street expectations.

The government’s closely watched jobs report for January has been pushed back from its scheduled release on Friday due to a four-day partial government shutdown that ended on Tuesday.

In the meantime, the ADP national employment report on Wednesday showed that U.S. private payrolls increased less than expected in January amid job losses in the professional and business services, as well as manufacturing sectors.

Advancing issues outnumbered falling ones within the S&P 500 by a 2.6-to-one ratio.

The S&P 500 posted 93 new highs and 23 new lows; the Nasdaq recorded 218 new highs and 318 new lows.

title: Trading Day: Tech it to the limit
content:
ORLANDO, Florida, Feb 4 (Reuters) - U.S. stocks mostly fell on Wednesday, slammed by worries that the artificial intelligence revolution could pose an existential threat to businesses across many sectors, while oil rose sharply on reports that planned U.S.-Iran talks may collapse.

More on that below. In my column today I look at the recent wild ride in gold, which has seen the biggest one-day price drop since 1983, biggest rise since 2008, and highest volatility on record. This isn’t what buyers of the world’s safest asset signed up for, is it?

If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today. 1. As software stocks slump, investors debate AI’sexistential threat 2. Dip-buyers go missing as software selloff slams stocks 3. Investors ramp up bets on steeper yield curve underWarsh-led Fed 4. Warning from ’Down Under’ may unsettle the Fed: MikeDolan 5. EXCLUSIVE-BOJ won’t come to the rescue of aTakaichi-driven bond rout

Today’s Key Market Moves * STOCKS: S&P 500 -0.5%, Nasdaq -1.5%, Dow +0.5%. UK FTSE100 and Euro Stoxx close at record highs. * SECTORS/SHARES: S&P 500 tech -2%, energy +2%. Eli Lilly+10%, Super Micro Computer +14%, Alphabet slides 6% after Q4results but recovers, AMD -17%, Palantir -11%. * FX: Dollar rises, gains most vs SEK, GBP in G10 space;Chinese yuan strongest fix and spot since May 2023. * BONDS: 2-year Treasury yield -1bp, 30-year yield +1bp;curve steepening grinds on. * COMMODITIES/METALS: Oil +3% on U.S.-Iran tensions,silver +3%, gold little changed, copper -3%.

Today’s Talking Points

* AI clouds darken

AI optimism is turning to disruption fear. U.S. and world markets are sliding on growing concern that artificial intelligence will have a significantly detrimental impact on a range of software-intensive businesses, from finance to law and coding.

The idea that AI’s rising tide will lift all boats is evaporating. Investors will have to pick winners and eschew losers, and determine where AI will enhance and where it will disrupt. As this is a brave new world, it’s really a guessing game. The only certainty? More volatility.

* Global growth tracking 3%

It’s not just the U.S. - the latest PMI figures from around the world show business activity has got off to a solid start this year. Manufacturing, in particular, is accelerating, and strong new orders suggest this momentum can be sustained.

There are pockets of concern, namely sluggish employment and high prices, and Europe is underperforming. But overall, output is holding up well and is consistent with global GDP growth of 3.0%, according to JPMorgan economists. That’s decent.

* Fed dove clips own wings

Fed Chair Jerome Powell reiterated last week that no one on the rate-setting FOMC has a rate hike as their next move "base case". But the hard growth and activity data, financial conditions, and above-target inflation all suggest further easing shouldn’t really be anyone’s base case either.

To be sure, markets aren’t contemplating a hike at all, and rates futures pricing still implies two 25 bps cuts this year. But if arch-dove Governor Stephen Miran is softening his stance a bit, calling for 100 bps of cuts this year rather than 150 bps a month ago, how close might the FOMC consensus be to flipping?

What could move markets tomorrow? * Australia trade (December) * Taiwan inflation (January) * Indonesia GDP (Q4) * European Central Bank interest rate decision * Euro zone retail sales (December) * Bank of England interest rate decision * Mexico interest rate decision * Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem speaks * U.S. weekly jobless claims * U.S. "JOLTS" job openings (December) * U.S. Challenger layoffs (January) * Global earnings including Amazon, Shell, Sony, KKR * U.S. Federal Reserve officials scheduled to speak includeAtlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic

Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here.

Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles, is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

(By Jamie McGeever; Editing by Nia Williams)

title: TSX gains amid gold rebound, earnings wave
content:
Investing.com - Canada’s main stock index ended higher on Wednesday, with a rebound in gold prices and a wave of corporate earnings.

The commodities-heavy S&P/TSX composite index gained 0.54% at 32,564.13.

Index rose by 0.6% to 32,388.60 on Tuesday, as a recovery from a slump to a four-week low last week continued.

Materials, which include metal mining shares, were buoyed by a bounce in gold and silver prices. A climb in oil prices also aided energy stocks.

But the gains were tempered by a sharp decline in data publisher Thomson Reuters, which was hit by the release of a new tool from artificial intelligence group Anthropic that helps with legal analysis.

U.S. stocks mixed

The NASDAQ Composite was lower by 1.4% to 22,796.56 points, having fallen as much as 2.5%. The benchmark S&P 500 index was down 0.3% to 6,894.60 points.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average, after a short bout of negative trading, was back in the green, last up 0.4% to 49,434.60.

Sentiment around software stocks has recently been dour, with investors fretting over competition from new AI models on the sector.

Alphabet earnings in the spotlight

Investors rotated out of richly valued tech names amid rising concerns over the disruptive impact of artificial intelligence on established software and analytics businesses.

With this in mind, investors are cautiously awaiting earnings from tech titan Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) after the closing bell.

Much of the attention is likely to swirl around the Google owner’s expensive foray into AI. Like many of its mega-cap tech peers, Alphabet has outlined plans to spend heavily on its AI ambitions, setting aside billions of dollars for the construction of the data centers and chips underpinning the nascent technology.

In the last three months of 2025, Alphabet’s shares spiked by around 29%, thanks largely to an upbeat reception for its newest Gemini AI model and a deal with Apple to help power the iPhone-maker’s Siri voice assistant.

Also in the spotlight, Advanced Micro Devices shares fell sharply premarket as its forecast for the first quarter disappointed, even after the chipmaking stock reported a record $10.3 billion in fourth-quarter revenue.

Restaurant chain Chipotle Mexican Grill shares also dropped after reporting falling customer traffic for a fourth consecutive quarter.

ADP payrolls, ISM non-manufacturing PMI due

Away from the corporate sector, investors will also focus on the release of economic data as they try to gauge the underlying strength of the U.S. economy, the largest in the world.

A brief government shutdown means that the widely-watched monthly jobs report was postponed earlier this week, meaning that ADP payrolls, due later in the session, will offer the best read on January labor market conditions.

"Consensus for ADP is 45k, which should help build confidence in an equally decent official payroll figure," said analysts at ING, in a note. "Considering the Fed’s more hawkish stance at the January meeting, some clear deterioration in the jobs market would be needed for a cut to be priced before June."

Also due for release will be a tracker of January activity in the U.S. services sector, which accounts for more than two-thirds of U.S. output. The Institute for Supply Management’s non-manufacturing purchasing managers’ index is seen coming in at 53.5, down from 54.4. A figure above 50 typically indicates expansion in services.

Gold climbs back towards $5,100

In commodity markets, gold prices rose back towards $5,100 an ounce on Wednesday amid renewed tensions between the U.S. and Iran that drummed up safe-haven demand for bullion.

The yellow metal extended gains after rebounding sharply from recent losses on Tuesday.

Crude rises on increased Iran tensions

Oil prices also gained Wednesday after the U.S. shot down an Iranian drone approaching a U.S. aircraft carrier in the Arabian Sea, while a group of Iranian gunboats were also seen approaching a U.S.-flagged tanker in the Strait of Hormuz.

This lifted concerns that an escalation in tension between Washington and Tehran could result in a disruption of supplies from this crude-rich region.

Brent oil futures for April rose 0.3% to $67.49 a barrel and West Texas Intermediate crude futures rose 0.4% to $63.44 a barrel. Both benchmarks rose nearly 2% on Tuesday.

title: Nasdaq falls 1.5% amid heavy rotation out of tech stocks; Amgen boosts Dow
content:
Investing.com -- Wall Street ended largely in the red on Wednesday, though the three main averages closed well off their session lows, as technology stocks extended their slide, weighed down by a post-earnings plunge in chipmaker AMD (NASDAQ:AMD).

Market participants also parsed some soft labor market data, while looking ahead to key earnings from Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) after the close.

The NASDAQ Composite slipped 1.5% to conclude at 22,904.58 points, having fallen as much as 2.5% earlier in the day. The benchmark S&P 500 index fell 0.5% to finish at 6,882.76 points.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average bucked the trend, gaining 0.5% to settle at 49,500.90 points. The blue-chip gauge was helped by a surge in drugmaker Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) after its quarterly results.

Alphabet earnings in the spotlight

Sentiment around software stocks has recently been dour, with investors fretting over competition from new AI models on the sector. Investors have rotated out of richly valued tech names amid rising concerns over the disruptive impact of artificial intelligence on established software and analytics businesses. The technology sector slumped in Tuesday’s session.

"The rotation away from the technology sector and into former laggard sectors like materials, consumer staples, and energy has continued to begin February," Will Tamplin, senior analyst at Fairlead Strategies, told Investing.com.

"Our rotational work suggests that the shift in leadership to start the year is meaningful. The underperformance from technology is a drag on the S&P 500 since it makes up nearly a third of the index. Our short-term indicators have seen minor deterioration for the SPX. We are watching support near 6,730, a breakdown below which would increase risk of a deeper correction. Secondary support is at the 200-day MA (6,445)," Tamplin added.

Investors are now cautiously awaiting earnings from tech titan Alphabet after the closing bell.

Much of the attention is likely to swirl around the Google owner’s expensive foray into AI. Like many of its mega-cap tech peers, Alphabet has outlined plans to spend heavily on its AI ambitions, setting aside billions of dollars for the construction of the data centers and chips underpinning the nascent technology.

The AI trade remained subdued on Wednesday, as AMD’s stock plunged 17% after its forecast for the first quarter disappointed, even after the chipmaker reported a record $10.3 billion in fourth-quarter revenue.

In other earnings moves, Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) stock soared after the drugmaker offered up an upbeat sales forecast for the year, boosted by strong demand for its weight loss drugs.

Amgen advanced after the Dow 30 component delivered a top-and-bottom line beat and continued to express confidence in its experimental weight loss drug MariTide.

ADP payrolls increase by less than expected

Away from the corporate sector, data released earlier in the session showed that U.S. private payrolls increased less than expected in January amid job losses in the professional and business services as well as manufacturing sectors.

Private employment rose by 22,000 jobs last month after a downwardly revised 37,000 increase in December, the ADP’s national employment report showed, below the 50,000 jobs gain expected.

A brief government shutdown means that the widely-watched monthly jobs report was postponed earlier this week, meaning that these ADP numbers currently offer the best read on January labor market conditions.

Also on the docket was a tracker of January activity in the U.S. services sector, which accounts for more than two-thirds of U.S. output. The Institute for Supply Management’s non-manufacturing purchasing managers’ index came in unchanged at 53.8, above the consensus. A figure above 50 typically indicates expansion in services.

Trump, Xi phone call

On a day packed with earnings and economic data, market participants also digested latest developments between the U.S. and China.

President Donald Trump on his Truth Social service said he had held a call with China’s President Xi Jinping, covering several topics from trade to Taiwan to the purchase of oil and gas by China from the U.S. and the consideration of buying additional agricultural products.

"The relationship with China, and my personal relationship with President Xi, is an extremely good one, and we both realize how important it is to keep it that way," Trump said.

Trump also mentioned his upcoming trip to China in April.

Gold gives up gains, oil pops

In commodity markets, gold prices were largely flat to lower after a rebound saw the yellow metal rise back towards $5,100 an ounce amid renewed tensions between the U.S. and Iran that drummed up safe-haven demand for bullion.

The yellow metal, along with silver, saw a historic selloff last Friday, but staged a strong rally on Tuesday.

Oil prices popped after an Axios report said nuclear talks between the U.S. and Iran - scheduled for Friday - had been canceled. They then pared gains after news that the talks were back on.

Developments between the two nations remained at the top of mind after an overnight U.S. shooting of an Iranian drone. Additionally, a group of Iranian gunboats were seen approaching a U.S.-flagged tanker in the Strait of Hormuz.

Brent oil futures for April were last up 1.9% at $68.58 a barrel and West Texas Intermediate crude futures were up 1.8% at $64.37 a barrel.

Both benchmarks rose nearly 2% on Tuesday.

Ayushman Ojha and Peter Nurse contributed to this article

title: Buy the weakness in this major chip stock, Truist says
content:
Investing.com -- Truist told clients in a note to “buy the weakness” in one major chip stock after what it called a messy but ultimately encouraging fourth quarter, arguing that the company’s long-term earnings power far outweighs near-term imperfections.

As of 13:07 ET (18:07 GMT), AMD shares were down as much as 16.3%.

Analyst William Stein stated that AMD is growing earnings at a “~45% CAGR through CY30 and trades at only ~11x CY30 EPS power,” adding that “it’s still a BUY.”

Truist said Q4 results beat expectations and Q1 guidance came in higher, even though a large portion of the upside was tied to an unusual China-related factor.

According to the firm, “65% of the revenues and all of the EPS beat were from selling a written-down China SKU.”

Despite that distortion, Truist noted that AMD reiterated a “60% DC CAGR, 35% total sales CAGR,” which it believes leads to “over $20 of EPS in 2030.”

Stein said the foundation of its bullish thesis is “strong customer engagement,” noting that industry contacts have confirmed the momentum behind AMD’s data-center and AI road map.

In Q4, revenue of about $10.3 billion beat consensus by roughly 6%, driven by “~9% upside in both Datacenter and Client segments.”

Truist highlighted accelerating deployments of Instinct MI350 GPUs, strong adoption of 5th-generation Epyc CPUs and management’s expectation that data-center revenue can grow “more than 60% annually over the next 3–5 years.”

The firm acknowledged that OpEx remains a drag, but said management reinforced plans for operating leverage in 2026. Truist lifted its 2027 earnings estimate to $10.11 and raised its price target to $283.

“Buy the weakness as the long-term growth message overwhelms the imperfections in Q4,” declared Stein.

title: AMD shares plunge 16.7%, triggering intraday rebalance of ETPs
content:
DUBLIN - Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares dropped 16.7% to $201.76 on Wednesday, triggering an automatic intraday rebalance of Leverage Shares’ 3x AMD exchange-traded products.

The significant price decline activated the rebalancing mechanism for the Leverage Shares 3x AMD ETP (ISIN:XS2337090422) when the stock fell from its previous closing price of $242.11, according to a statement from Leverage Shares plc.

The price movement crossed the -16.67% threshold established in the methodology for the iSTOXX Leveraged 3x AMD Index, which tracks the performance of AMD shares with triple leverage.

Following the trigger at 11:40:32 EST, Leverage Shares initiated the rebalancing process five minutes later, executing it over a 10-minute period. During the rebalance, the issuer achieved an average price of $201.44 for the underlying shares, resulting in an ETP Security Value of $0.82680.

The rebalancing mechanism is designed to protect leveraged ETP holders during periods of extreme market volatility by resetting the leverage factor when significant price movements occur.

Leverage Shares’ ETPs provide investors with magnified exposure to the daily performance of individual stocks through a regulated exchange-traded structure.

The company did not provide information about what caused the sharp decline in AMD’s share price in its press release statement.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: Nvidia-rival Cerebras Systems valued at $23.1 billion in latest financing
content:
Feb 4 (Reuters) - AI chipmaker Cerebras Systems said on Wednesday it raised $1 billion in a late-stage funding round that valued it at $23 billion, nearly tripling its valuation just over four months after its previous financing.

The round was led by Tiger Global. Other investors included Benchmark, Coatue and Donald Trump Jr.-backed 1789 Capital.

AI-linked companies have continued to draw billions in private financing as corporations and governments race to develop data centers for supporting the still-nascent technology.

The race has made the chips required for computing power in these data centers a prized commodity, fueling rival Nvidia’s meteoric rise to the most valuable company in the world.

The fundraising marks Cerebras’ second billion-dollar round since September, when it was valued at $8.1 billion, reflecting the investor allure for a company that could grow to become a key player in the AI-chip supply chain alongside Nvidia and AMD, which also participated in the round.

It comes at a time when key AI players are looking to diversify their chip supplies.

Reuters reported on Monday that OpenAI was seeking alternatives to Nvidia for AI inference chips, including Cerebras, AMD, and Groq.

Amid OpenAI’s reservations, Nvidia approached companies working on SRAM-heavy chips, including Cerebras and Groq, about a potential acquisition. Cerebras declined and struck a commercial deal with OpenAI, which was announced last month.

The round is also Cerebras’ first since it withdrew its U.S. IPO filing in October, underscoring a broader trend of companies staying private for longer with abundant capital available outside of public markets.

Sunnyvale, California-based Cerebras is known for its wafer-scale engine chips, designed to speed up the training and inference of large AI models and compete with products from Nvidia and other AI chipmakers.

title: UBS lowers AMD stock price target to $310 on gaming business cut
content:
UBS expressed frustration that sustained periods of earnings beats and raises have been difficult for AMD, partly due to operating expenses that "tend to always surprise to the upside." The broader analyst community maintains a bullish stance on AMD, with consensus recommendations leaning toward "Buy" and price targets ranging from $210 to $380.

Despite the near-term challenges, UBS sees a "clear path" to earnings per share exceeding $11 in 2027 and $15 in 2028, which the firm believes should support higher stock prices in the future.

The firm expects AMD’s next opportunity for significant earnings beats may not arrive until later this year when the company’s MI455x chips begin ramping up production significantly in the fourth quarter.

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has reported its fourth quarter 2025 earnings, which showed a modest beat despite mixed investor sentiment, as noted by Raymond James. RBC Capital observed that the results were bolstered by unexpected China AI revenue, aligning with their preview. Meanwhile, Baird has reiterated an Outperform rating with a $300.00 price target, emphasizing the growth in AI-related x86 server CPU demand as a significant driver for AMD’s business. Benchmark also maintained a Buy rating, highlighting AMD’s expanding role in the AI Data Center market. TD Cowen expressed a positive outlook with a $290.00 price target, focusing on the upcoming MI450/Helios product launch despite a "noisy quarter." Concerns were noted by Raymond James regarding AMD’s reliance on the OpenAI project and potential bottlenecks in the MI450 ramp, although AMD management reassured that the MI450 is progressing with new customers. These developments reflect various analyst perspectives on AMD’s performance and potential in the AI sector.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: Benchmark reiterates Buy rating on AMD stock, citing AI growth
content:
Benchmark analyst Cody Acree expressed confidence in AMD’s "strong record results" and "attractive outlook," particularly noting the company’s "rapidly growing leverage to the aggressively expanding AI Data Center market" across both CPU and GPU product lines. This optimism aligns with AMD’s robust 31.83% revenue growth in the last twelve months, with analysts forecasting continued strong growth of 32% for fiscal year 2025.

The firm pointed to AMD’s growing traction with major partners including OpenAI, Oracle, Meta, and various Sovereign AI projects as evidence of improved competitive positioning against market leader Nvidia. With a market capitalization of $338.11 billion, AMD has established itself as a prominent player in the semiconductor industry, maintaining a solid financial foundation with a current ratio of 2.31.

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) reported its fourth-quarter 2025 earnings, which were mostly in line with expectations and included a modest beat, according to Raymond James. The company’s quarterly performance was bolstered by unexpected China AI revenue, as noted by RBC Capital. AMD also provided guidance for the upcoming March quarter with revenue projections slightly above consensus, estimated at $9.8 billion compared to the $9.4 billion consensus estimate.

Mizuho has adjusted its price target for AMD to $275 from $285, citing higher expenses while maintaining an Outperform rating. Baird reiterated its Outperform rating, highlighting growth prospects in AI-related business segments and expected share gains in the Client segment. TD Cowen maintained its $290 price target, focusing on the MI450/Helios product launch as a key factor for AMD’s long-term investment thesis. RBC Capital also maintained its $230 price target, reflecting a mixed outlook. Despite these developments, some investor concerns remain, particularly regarding AMD’s reliance on the OpenAI project and potential bottlenecks for the MI450 ramp.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: AMD, Eli Lilly among market cap stock movers on Wednesday

title: AMD stock price target maintained at $230 by RBC Capital on mixed outlook
content:
Investing.com - RBC Capital has reiterated its Sector Perform rating and $230.00 price target on AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) following the company’s fourth-quarter 2025 results. This target aligns closely with AMD’s current trading price of $208.42, with analyst targets across Wall Street ranging from $210 to $380.

The semiconductor company reported quarterly performance that aligned with RBC’s preview, though the analyst noted that results were bolstered by China AI revenue that wasn’t originally included in their model. AMD’s revenue reached $32.03 billion over the last twelve months, with impressive growth of 31.83%.

AMD’s Data Center segment showed strength from server CPU demand and market share gains, while AI GPUs also grew quarter-over-quarter, even when excluding the China revenue contribution. The company maintains a healthy gross profit margin of 51.46%, reflecting its competitive position as a prominent player in the semiconductor industry.

RBC slightly raised its estimates for AMD, noting that higher operating expenses partially offset higher revenue projections, while maintaining that with AMD’s valuation at approximately 40% premium to NVIDIA, limited catalysts exist until OpenAI starts ramping in volume in the fourth quarter of 2026. This premium valuation is reflected in AMD’s high P/E ratio of 126.66 and EV/EBITDA multiple of 64.55.

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) reported quarterly earnings that were mostly in line with expectations and provided guidance for the upcoming quarter slightly above consensus at $9.8 billion, compared to the $9.4 billion estimate. Despite this, AMD’s stock experienced a decline of approximately 8% following its fourth-quarter earnings report, as noted by Raymond James, due to investor concerns regarding reliance on the OpenAI project. Rosenblatt Securities reiterated a Buy rating on AMD, highlighting strong data center demand and a price target of $300, emphasizing AMD’s market share gains in this sector. Mizuho adjusted its price target for AMD to $275, citing higher expenses but maintained an Outperform rating. Baird also reiterated an Outperform rating, pointing to positive developments in AMD’s AI-related business segments and expected share gains in the Client segment. Northland reaffirmed an Outperform rating with a $260 price target, acknowledging robust momentum in AMD’s AI products and strong demand in the personal computer segment. These developments reflect a mix of optimism and caution among analysts regarding AMD’s future prospects.

title: AMD shares slump as guidance fails to impress amid weak sentiment for tech stocks
content:
Investing.com -- AMD stock slumped on Wednesday, a day after the company’s forecast of a sequential decline in revenue clouded sentiment already weighed down by a rotation out of the heavyweight technology sector.

AMD beat Q4 2025 top-and-bottom line estimates, but guided for a Q/Q decline of about 5% in revenue for the current quarter.

Shares of the company slid 12.8% after the opening bell. The stock was among the top percentage losers on the tech-heavy NASDAQ Composite.

Santa Clara, Calif.-based AMD is one of the big players in the U.S. semiconductor market. It designs and develops high-performance chip components such as graphics processing units (GPUs), central processing units (CPUs), field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs), and system on a chip (SoC) units that power personal computers, data centers, gaming consoles, and artificial intelligence processes.

AMD lags market leader NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) in terms of sales, though in terms of stock performance, it has outstripped its rival. Shares of AMD climbed 77.3% in 2025, compared to Nvidia’s 38.9%. In terms of the YTD race, AMD is up 13.1% to Nvidia’s 3.3% decline.

"We believe investors have been girding for a strong print, which we got. However, it seems a bit of a kerfuffle that in such an intense environment, overall results weren’t all that much beyond ’inline’ without the China boost," Bernstein analysts led by Stacy Rasgon said in a research note on Wednesday, referring to $390 million in quarterly revenue the company booked from sales of its AMD Instinct MI308 AI accelerator series to China.

AMD’s results come at a time when the artificial intelligence trade has been taking it on the chin, with investors once again questioning the elevated valuations and high spending plans by heavyweight technology firms. In such an environment, anything short of blowout guidance was unlikely to impress investors gripped in a rotation out of technology stocks.

"On the positive, server trends admittedly appear very solid, and client share gains continue. On the other hand near-term AI numbers are not really inflecting (outside of China that no one really wants to pay for) and the pace and trajectory of the OpenAI ramp remains a bit up for debate (at a minimum it clearly seems increasingly likely to be a Q4 volume game), and client headwinds may materialize into the 2H (alongside console headwinds that are happening now)," the Bernstein analysts said.

"This is the problem when a stock sports a triple-digit P/E – it’s priced for perfection and anything that can be construed as less than perfect leaves a company vulnerable if their guidance is merely good, not great," Steve Sosnick, chief strategist at Interactive Brokers, told Investing.com on Tuesday.

Looking at its results, AMD earned $1.53 per share on an adjusted basis on revenue of $10.27 billion for Q4 2025. Analysts had been expecting a profit of $1.32 per share on revenue of $9.64 billion.

In terms of guidance, AMD sees Q1 2026 revenue of $9.50 billion to $10.10 billion, versus a consensus of $9.37 billion. The outlook represents Y/Y growth of approximately 32% and a Q/Q decline of about 5%.

"We are entering 2026 with strong momentum across our business, led by accelerating adoption of our high-performance EPYC and Ryzen CPUs and the rapid scaling of our data center AI franchise," top boss Lisa Su said in a statement.

Turning to AMD’s segments, its data-center business, seen by analysts and investors as the firm’s biggest driver, pulled in record revenue of $5.4 billion in Q4 2025, up 39% Y/Y. AMD’s EPYC processors and Instinct GPUs are popular products to power cloud computing and AI workloads.

Meanwhile, AMD’s client and gaming business, which caters to individual consumers, gamers, and corporate clients, brought in revenue of $3.9 billion, up 37% Y/Y. AMD’s Ryzen processors and Radeon graphic cards are the flagship products of this segment.

Finally, AMD’s embedded segment reported revenue of $950 million, up 3% Y/Y.

title: AMD, Uber, Chipotle and Novo Nordisk fell premarket; Alphabet, Eli Lilly rose
content:
Investing.com -- U.S. stock futures edged higher Wednesday, with the tech sector in the spotlight as the earnings season continues in full flow.

Here are some of the biggest premarket U.S. stock movers today:

Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) stock rose 1.3% ahead of the Google parent’s quarterly earnings, with most of the attention likely to swirl around its expensive foray into AI.

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) stock slumped 9.5% after the semiconductor forecast a slight dip in quarterly revenue, rekindling doubts over how effectively it can take on Nvidia in the red-hot AI market.

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) stock soared 6.5% after the drugmaker offered up an upbeat sales forecast for the year, boosted by strong demand for its weight loss drug.

Chipotle Mexican Grill (NYSE:CMG) stock fell 5.5% after the Mexican-style restaurant chain reported falling traffic for the fourth straight quarter and projected flat same-store sales growth for 2026.

GE HealthCare Technologies (NASDAQ:GEHC) stock gained 1.6% after the health technology company reported fourth-quarter results that beat expectations, and offered 2026 profit guidance that also exceeded consensus estimates.

Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) stock gained 10% after the tech company raised its annual revenue forecast on sustained demand for its AI-optimized servers as companies ramp up data-center capacity.

Match Group (NASDAQ:MTCH) stock gained 7.3% after the online dating company, which owns Tinder, forecast first-quarter revenue above estimates, signaling early gains from its turnaround push.

Take-Two Interactive Software (NASDAQ:TTWO) stock gained 5.1% after the video game publisher raised its annual bookings forecast, betting on strong sales of its premium sports, action and mobile titles as it gears up to launch the highly anticipated "Grand Theft Auto VI" on November 19.

Novo Nordisk (NYSE:NVO) stock dropped 3% after the Wegovy-maker issued a sales warning for 2026, citing lower prices due to the "Most Favoured Nations" agreement in the U.S. as well as competition.

Mondelez (NASDAQ:MDLZ) stock fell 4.2% after the Cadbury-parent forecast a subdued year ahead, as price increases are turning away cost‑conscious shoppers.

Stanley Black & Decker (NYSE:SWK) stock fell 3% after the  tools and outdoor solutions provider’s revenue fell short of expectations as North American retail softness weighed on sales.

title: Evercore ISI raises AMD stock price target to $328 on data center growth
content:
The price target increase comes despite AMD shares falling 8% in after-hours trading following the company’s recent earnings report, which Evercore described as a "beat/raise" quarter. With a beta of 1.95, AMD stock typically experiences more pronounced price movements than the broader market.

Evercore cited management’s conviction that data center revenues can grow more than 60% in 2026 as a key factor in its bullish outlook, along with GPU revenues excluding China increasing approximately 20% quarter-over-quarter in the December quarter. This aligns with AMD’s impressive revenue growth of 31.83% over the last twelve months.

The research firm noted that eight of the top 10 AI companies are using AMD GPUs, and purchase commitments are expected to increase significantly each quarter throughout 2026, indicating strong visibility for the company’s products.

Evercore’s new price target reflects 32 times its 2027 earnings per share estimate of $11.27 for AMD, discounted back one year.

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) reported its fourth-quarter results, surpassing consensus estimates with a revenue of $10.3 billion, an adjusted gross margin of 57.0%, and adjusted earnings per share of $1.53. These figures exceeded analyst expectations of $9.7 billion in revenue, a 54.5% gross margin, and earnings per share of $1.32. Following this performance, several analyst firms made adjustments to their price targets for AMD. BofA Securities raised its price target to $280, citing AMD’s role as a credible alternative AI GPU supplier to Nvidia. Truist Securities also increased its target to $283, highlighting the company’s growth outlook. Meanwhile, Raymond James lowered its target to $365 due to concerns about GPU sales to China, despite acknowledging a solid fourth quarter and strong guidance for the first quarter of 2026. Loop Capital reiterated its Buy rating with a $310 price target, and Stifel maintained its $280 target, both expressing confidence in AMD’s strong results. These developments reflect ongoing analyst interest and varied perspectives on AMD’s future performance.

title: Stifel reiterates Buy rating on AMD stock, maintains $280 price target
content:
AMD reported fourth-quarter results that exceeded consensus estimates, with revenue of $10.3 billion, adjusted gross margin of 57.0%, and adjusted earnings per share of $1.53, compared to analyst expectations of $9.7 billion, 54.5%, and $1.32, respectively. This performance aligns with AMD’s impressive 31.8% revenue growth over the last twelve months, reaching $32.03 billion.

The company’s performance was boosted by $390 million in MI308 revenue from China and accounting tailwinds, including a $360 million inventory reserve release related to previously written-down China inventory, according to Stifel analyst Ruben Roy.

For the first quarter, AMD provided revenue guidance of $9.8 billion, above the consensus estimate of $9.4 billion, with $100 million attributed to non-recurring China revenue. The company projected an adjusted gross margin of 55.0%, relatively in line with the 54.7% consensus estimate. AMD maintains a strong financial position with a current ratio of 2.31, indicating liquid assets comfortably exceed short-term obligations.

In other recent news, AMD reported strong fourth-quarter results that exceeded expectations, with significant contributions from a written-down China SKU. The company’s first-quarter guidance also surpassed projections, leading to positive reactions from analysts. Truist Securities raised its price target for AMD to $283, maintaining a Buy rating due to the impressive earnings and revenue performance. Similarly, Piper Sandler reiterated an Overweight rating with a $300 price target, highlighting the company’s strong quarterly performance and optimistic revenue guidance.

Bernstein also adjusted its price target for AMD to $235, noting solid server trends and client share gains, though it maintained a Market Perform rating. KeyBanc Capital Markets increased its price target to $300, emphasizing the strong growth in AMD’s data center segment, which saw substantial quarterly and yearly increases. Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs maintained a Neutral rating with a $210 price target, pointing out that despite strong revenue and guidance, higher-than-expected operating expenses could impact future performance. These recent developments provide a comprehensive view of AMD’s current market position and analyst expectations.

title: AMD stock rating reiterated at Buy by Loop Capital on strong results
content:
Investing.com - Loop Capital has reiterated its Buy rating and $310.00 price target on AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) following the company’s fourth-quarter 2025 results and first-quarter 2026 guidance. This target aligns with the broader analyst community, which maintains a strong consensus recommendation of 1.55 (Buy) with price targets ranging from $210 to $380.

The price target assumes AMD shares will trade at approximately 29.0x Loop Capital’s revised fiscal year 2027 earnings per share estimate. Currently, AMD trades at a considerably higher P/E ratio of 126.66, reflecting the market’s strong growth expectations for the semiconductor giant, which now commands a $394 billion market capitalization.

Loop Capital highlighted that AMD continues to rapidly gain market share from Intel, particularly in server products but also in personal computers.

The firm noted AMD is building momentum with its artificial intelligence strategy, including software and systems capabilities that incorporate Instinct MI355 GPUs affecting the second half of fiscal year 2025 and MI400 Helios in the second half of fiscal year 2026.

Loop Capital also addressed that, contrary to some speculation, AMD’s Helios and MI450 products remain on schedule for the second half of fiscal year 2026, while sales into China are beginning to increase modestly, currently excluding the MI325 product for which AMD is seeking an export license.

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) reported fourth-quarter results that surpassed consensus estimates, with revenue reaching $10.3 billion and an adjusted gross margin of 57.0%. The company’s adjusted earnings per share came in at $1.53, exceeding analyst expectations of $9.7 billion in revenue, a 54.5% gross margin, and $1.32 per share. Truist Securities raised its price target for AMD to $283.00, citing the company’s strong fourth-quarter results and optimistic first-quarter guidance. Stifel reiterated its Buy rating with a $280.00 price target, while Piper Sandler maintained an Overweight rating, setting a target of $300.00 based on AMD’s robust quarterly performance.

Bernstein increased its price target to $235.00, highlighting AMD’s solid server trends and client share gains. Goldman Sachs, however, maintained a Neutral rating with a $210.00 price target, noting that higher-than-expected operating expenses might offset strong revenue and guidance from the Datacenter segment. These analyst actions reflect a mix of optimism and caution regarding AMD’s future performance. Despite varying opinions, the consensus acknowledges AMD’s recent financial success and strategic positioning.

title: AMD stock rating reiterated at Overweight by Piper Sandler on strong growth
content:
Investing.com - Piper Sandler has reiterated its Overweight rating and $300.00 price target on AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) following the company’s strong quarterly performance. This target represents a 24% upside from the current price of $242.11, with AMD’s market capitalization now standing at $394 billion.

AMD is experiencing significant acceleration across multiple business segments, including Server CPU, data center GPUs, Client, and Embedded divisions, with Piper Sandler expecting double-digit growth across all these segments through 2026. The company’s total revenue has reached $32.03 billion, though investors should note AMD trades at a high P/E ratio of 126.7.

The research firm specifically projects data center growth of approximately 62% year-over-year in 2026 and 66% year-over-year in 2027, largely driven by AMD’s deal with OpenAI.

Piper Sandler noted that additional multi-gigawatt deals, both public and non-public, could potentially contribute to AMD’s performance in the second half of 2026, suggesting their current projections "could prove to be conservative."

In other recent news, Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) reported impressive fourth-quarter 2025 earnings, surpassing analysts’ expectations. The company achieved an earnings per share (EPS) of $1.53, exceeding the forecasted $1.32, while revenue reached $10.3 billion, outpacing the anticipated $9.64 billion. KeyBanc Capital Markets responded to these strong results by raising its price target for AMD to $300, citing robust growth in the data center segment, which saw a 24% increase quarter-over-quarter and a 39% rise year-over-year. Meanwhile, Goldman Sachs maintained a Neutral rating on AMD with a price target of $210, noting that despite strong revenue and guidance, higher-than-expected operating expenses could impact future performance. These developments reflect significant interest and varied perspectives from analysts on AMD’s potential growth trajectory.

title: AMD’s Frankfurt-listed shares fall after weaker sales
content:
Feb 4 (Reuters) - Advanced Micro Devices shares fell over 6% in early Frankfurt trading on Wednesday after the company forecast a slight decline in quarterly revenue, raising concerns about whether it can effectively challenge Nvidia in the booming AI market.

The stock ended Tuesday’s regular session down 1.7%. The shares have risen 13.1% so far this year

title: Wall Street ends sharply down as traders fret about AI disruption
content:
By Noel Randewich and Twesha Dikshit

Feb 3 (Reuters) - Wall Street ended sharply lower on Tuesday as investors worried about AI creating more competition for software makers, keeping them on edge ahead of quarterly reports from Alphabet and Amazon later this week.

AI heavyweights Nvidia and Microsoft each fell almost 3%. Alphabet dropped 1.2% ahead of its report on Wednesday, while Amazon declined 1.8% ahead of its Thursday report.

Investors in recent months have become pickier about AI-related stocks, looking for companies generating measurable returns from their outsized investments in the new technology.

Wall Street’s attention on Tuesday turned to technology companies that could face steeper competition and lower margins as a result of AI. One catalyst driving those concerns was Anthropic’s launch of a legal tool for its Claude AI chatbot.

“We’re looking at a lot of software names that are seen as companies that may well be disrupted when we start to see the advancement of artificial intelligence. We’re seeing a lot of software companies across the spectrum get hit,” said Art Hogan, chief market strategist at B. Riley Wealth.

Salesforce, Datadog and Adobe lost about 7%, Synopsys and Atlassian fell about 8%, and Intuit slumped 11%.

AI data firm Palantir bucked the trend, rallying almost 7% after strong quarterly results late on Monday.

The S&P 500 software and services index fell 3.8%, down for a fifth consecutive day.

"We’ve got an expensive market and expectations are really high. Many areas, especially around AI, are priced for perfection. That’s just got us in a skittish environment," said John Campbell, senior portfolio manager, Allspring Global Investments.

Healthcare stocks came under pressure after Wegovy maker Novo Nordisk warned that it expected a steep decline in annual sales. The company’s U.S.-listed shares plummeted nearly 15%.

Rival Eli Lilly fell 3.9%, while obesity drugmaker Structure Therapeutics lost 6.75%.

Walmart climbed about 3% to become the first brick-and-mortar retailer ever to hit $1 trillion in stock market value.

Advanced Micro Devices fell 1.7% ahead of its quarterly report after the bell.

Walt Disney dipped 0.2% after it named theme parks head Josh D’Amaro as CEO, placing a longtime insider at the helm and ending succession uncertainty.

PayPal slumped 20% after it forecast 2026 profit below estimates.

The S&P 500 declined 0.84% to end the session at 6,917.81 points.

The Nasdaq declined 1.43% to 23,255.19 points, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average declined 0.34% to 49,240.99 points.

Even as the S&P 500 ended lower, advancing issues outnumbered falling ones within the index by a 1.2-to-one ratio.

With Tuesday’s losses, the S&P 500 is up about 1% in 2026 and the Nasdaq is flat.

Volume on U.S. exchanges was heavy, with 23.5 billion shares traded, compared to an average of 19.6 billion shares over the previous 20 sessions.

Six of the 11 S&P 500 sector indexes declined, led lower by information technology, down 2.17%, followed by a 1.28% loss in communication services.

EARNINGS DELUGE

With one quarter of the S&P 500 set to report quarterly results this week, analysts expect companies to have grown their earnings nearly 11% in the December quarter, up from an estimate of about 9% at the start of January, according to LSEG data.

Pfizer fell 3.3% despite posting fourth-quarter profit above estimates, while Merck rose 2.2% after its quarterly results.

PepsiCo gained 4.9% after the company announced price cuts on core brands such as Lay’s and Doritos.

Meanwhile, legislation to end a U.S. government shutdown narrowly cleared a procedural hurdle in the House of Representatives, setting up a vote on final passage later in the day.

The partial shutdown has postponed releases of key jobs data on Friday along with the JOLTS report, originally expected on Tuesday.

The S&P 500 posted 81 new highs and 28 new lows; the Nasdaq recorded 202 new highs and 311 new lows.

title: AMD Q4 2025 slides: record revenue and earnings as AI demand surges
content:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) reported record financial results for the fourth quarter and full year 2025 in its February 3, 2026 presentation, highlighting strong growth across its key business segments, particularly in data center and AI markets.

Executive Summary

AMD delivered record quarterly revenue of $10.3 billion in Q4 2025, representing a 34% year-over-year increase. This impressive performance was driven by strong demand for the company’s data center products, including EPYC processors and Instinct GPUs, as well as robust growth in client and gaming segments.

As shown in the following chart of quarterly revenue growth:

The company’s non-GAAP earnings per share reached $1.53, up 40% year-over-year, significantly exceeding analyst expectations of $1.32. This earnings beat helped drive AMD’s stock up 1.8% in after-hours trading to $250.70, reflecting positive investor sentiment about the company’s performance and outlook.

Quarterly Performance Highlights

AMD’s gross margin showed substantial improvement in Q4 2025, with GAAP gross margin increasing to 54% (up 3 percentage points year-over-year) and non-GAAP gross margin reaching 57% (also up 3 percentage points). The company noted that its non-GAAP gross margin benefited from a favorable product mix and the release of inventory reserves related to AMD Instinct MI308 GPU products.

The following chart illustrates AMD’s gross margin improvement:

Operating income also saw significant growth, with GAAP operating income more than doubling to $1.8 billion (up 101% year-over-year) and non-GAAP operating income increasing to $2.9 billion (up 41%). This growth was primarily driven by higher revenue, partially offset by increased operating expenses.

AMD’s operating income growth is visualized in this chart:

The company’s diluted earnings per share reflected this strong operational performance, with GAAP EPS of $0.92 (up 217% year-over-year) and non-GAAP EPS of $1.53 (up 40%).

Segment Performance

AMD’s Data Center segment was a standout performer, with Q4 revenue of $5.4 billion, up 39% year-over-year. This growth was driven by strong demand for AMD EPYC processors and the ramp of AMD Instinct GPUs. Operating income for this segment reached $1.8 billion, up 51% from the previous year.

The following chart details the Data Center segment’s performance and strategic highlights:

The Client and Gaming segment also delivered strong results, with revenue of $3.9 billion, up 37% year-over-year. This growth was primarily driven by record sales of Ryzen processors, higher semi-custom revenue, and strong demand for Radeon gaming GPUs. Operating income for this segment was $725 million, up 46% from the previous year.

As shown in the following segment breakdown:

The Embedded segment showed modest growth, with revenue of $950 million, up 3% year-over-year. Operating income was $357 million, slightly down from $362 million in the previous year, primarily due to product mix changes.

Full Year 2025 Performance

For the full year 2025, AMD achieved record revenue of $34.6 billion, up 34% from 2024. This strong annual performance was driven by growth in the Data Center and Client and Gaming segments.

The following chart illustrates AMD’s annual revenue growth:

Annual operating income showed significant improvement, with GAAP operating income of $3.7 billion (up 94% year-over-year) and non-GAAP operating income of $7.8 billion (up 27%).

Full-year diluted EPS also saw substantial growth, with GAAP EPS of $2.65 (up 165% year-over-year) and non-GAAP EPS of $4.17 (up 26%).

Financial Position

AMD ended Q4 2025 with a strong balance sheet, including $10.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, up 46% from the previous quarter. The company generated record cash from operating activities of $2.3 billion and record free cash flow of $2.1 billion during the quarter.

Strategic Initiatives

AMD’s presentation highlighted several strategic initiatives that are driving its growth and competitive positioning:

1. Extending compute technology leadership across CPU, GPU, FPGA, networking, and system-on-chip solutions

2. Expanding data center leadership in cloud, enterprise, and rack-scale solutions

3. Developing open software platforms and enabling developers with the AMD ROCm software stack

4. Powering AI everywhere with high-performance, adaptive, and custom AI platforms

The company also announced several strategic partnerships and product launches during the quarter, including:

Forward-Looking Statements

For Q1 2026, AMD provided the following guidance (on a non-GAAP basis):

The company’s financial outlook is summarized in the following slide:

Conclusion

AMD’s Q4 and full-year 2025 results demonstrate the company’s strong execution and growing competitive position in key markets, particularly in data center and AI. With record revenue, earnings, and cash flow, AMD is well-positioned to continue its momentum into 2026, driven by its expanding product portfolio and strategic focus on high-performance computing and AI.

The stock’s positive after-hours reaction, rising 1.8% to $250.70, reflects investor confidence in AMD’s strategy and growth prospects. As the company continues to invest in AI capabilities and expand its data center presence, it appears well-positioned to capitalize on what CEO Lisa Su described as "a multi-year demand supercycle for high performance and AI computing."
